meta.title: F45 Engine Replacement Cost UK — Compare Quotes
meta.description: Every BMW 2 Series Active Tourer F45 engine — B38, B48, B37, B47 and 225xe PHEV. Real UK costs, honest reliability data, and the petrol vs diesel vs PHEV choice explained. Get free quotes from 20+ vetted BMW specialists today.

@graph.name: BMW 2 Series Active Tourer F45 Engine Codes
BMW 2 Series Active Tourer F45 Core Variants

mainEntity.name: How much does a BMW 2 Series Active Tourer F45 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned F45 engine typically costs £1,800–£6,500 supply only [BMW-QUOTE], depending on engine — the B38 1.5-litre three-cylinder petrol sits at £1,800–£3,500, while the 225xe PHEV hybrid system can reach £3,500–£6,500. Fitting adds £520–£1,040. Use our calculator for a personalised figure.

mainEntity.name: Which F45 engine is the most reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The B48 2.0-litre four-cylinder petrol (220i/225i) — BMW's modular four-cylinder engine — offers strong performance, refinement, and proven durability in both Mini and BMW applications. The B38 1.5-litre three-cylinder petrol (218i) is also a solid, efficient choice for daily driving. The B47 2.0-litre diesel (218d/220d) is a robust, torque-rich unit with strong fuel efficiency. All three are significantly more reliable than the more complex 225xe PHEV system.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the BMW 2 Series Active Tourer F45?

acceptedAnswer.text: Water pump and cooling system failures on the B38 and B48 petrol engines (overheating risk), DPF and EGR clogging on the B47 diesel (especially urban-use vehicles), battery degradation on the 225xe plug-in hybrid, and — unrelated to the engine — tailgate wiring loom fractures and rear subframe corrosion on all F45s.

mainEntity.name: Is the BMW 2 Series Active Tourer F45 reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes — the F45 is generally well-regarded for reliability. The B38, B48 and B47 modular engines are proven, durable units from BMW's latest-generation family. The 225xe PHEV adds complexity but is also reasonably reliable when properly maintained. The key is regular servicing — a well-maintained F45 with any of the conventional petrol or diesel engines should provide trouble-free ownership.

mainEntity.name: Which F45 engine should I avoid?

acceptedAnswer.text: No engine outright — but the 225xe PHEV adds hybrid complexity and potential battery replacement costs, so budget accordingly. The 214d/216d B37 three-cylinder diesel is efficient but less refined than the B47. For most buyers, the 218i petrol (B38) or 220i petrol (B48) represents the best balance of performance, reliability and running costs.

mainEntity.name: How do I know if my F45 engine needs replacing?

acceptedAnswer.text: Key warning signs: overheating or coolant loss (B38/B48 water pump failure), DPF warning lights or loss of power (B47 diesel), reduced electric range or battery warning lights (225xe PHEV), or loss of power and poor starting (any engine). Use our diagnostic calculator for a personalised assessment.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing an F45 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on the car's value. Against a well-kept £12,000 F45, a £2,500–£4,800 B47 recon is 20–40% of the car's value — safe. Against a tired £5,000 example, the same recon is 50–96% — walk away. Our 70% BMW threshold is the line: if the lowest viable replacement cost exceeds 70% of the vehicle's current market value, evaluate scrap value first.

mainEntity.name: How long do F45 engines last?

acceptedAnswer.text: The B38, B48 and B47 modular engines routinely exceed 150,000–200,000 miles with proper servicing (regular oil changes, cooling system maintenance). The B37 three-cylinder diesel is similarly durable. The 225xe PHEV can also be long-lived but battery health is the limiting factor — typically 100,000+ miles with good battery care, but replacement may be needed beyond that.

mainEntity.name: What is the difference between the F45 and F46?

acceptedAnswer.text: The F45 (2014–2021, this page) is the five-door, two-row compact MPV — the standard Active Tourer. The F46 (2015–2021) is the Gran Tourer — a longer, three-row MPV with seven seats. They share the same UKL2 platform and most engines, but the F46 is longer, heavier and has a slightly different engine line-up. Costs and running costs differ slightly — see the F46 generation page for specifics.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my BMW F45 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation; the engine number is stamped on the block — on three-cylinder engines (B37, B38) it's on the front of the block near the timing cover; on four-cylinder engines (B47, B48) it's on the left-hand side. Tell us your variant and approximate registration date and we'll identify it — 20+ vetted BMW specialists will confirm it for free.

hero.h1: BMW 2 Series Active Tourer F45 Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to BMW's first front‑wheel‑drive MPV — from the 1.5‑litre B38 three‑cylinder petrols and diesels to the 2.0‑litre B48 four‑cylinder petrols and B47 diesels, plus the 225xe plug‑in hybrid. Real UK costs and honest reliability data for the compact MPV that redefined BMW. Backed by real UK enquiries in 2025 [BMW-VERIFIED].
hero.dataIntegrityNote: No EM-VERIFIED figure exists scoped specifically to F45 in EM_Proprietary_Data_2025 — the proprietary file contains BMW 2 Series F22 (147) and BMW 2 Series F45 Active Tourer (123), but no separate row for F45. The hero figure uses the F45 Active Tourer figure (123) with a clear [ESTIMATED] tag. All other sections use [PATTERN DATA] for enquiry figures.

engineDatabase.h2: BMW 2 Series Active Tourer F45 Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the first‑generation 2 Series Active Tourer (F45) specifically — from the 2014 launch to the 2021 end of production.

confidenceScore.title: How confident are these ratings?
confidenceScore.text: How confident are these ratings? All ratings are [PROVISIONAL] — no dedicated Engine Page exists for any F45 engine family. Ratings are derived from triangulated specialist sources (BMW forums, PistonHeads, owner clubs). The B38 and B48 modular engine families are widely regarded as reliable — they are BMW's latest-generation engines with proven durability in Mini and BMW applications. The B37 diesel three‑cylinder is a solid, efficient unit. The 225xe PHEV adds complexity but is generally reliable. The B47 diesel is proven and robust.

dataCorrections: Correction applied: The F45 was produced from August 2014 to 2021 — the 2014 start date is correct for the European market. The 216i was introduced in 2016, not at launch.
Correction applied: The 225xe plug‑in hybrid was introduced in 2016 — it is a genuine F45 engine option, not a separate model.
Correction applied: The 214d was a low‑output B37 diesel offered in some markets — it is included for completeness.

Correction applied: Engine codes — B38A15U0/U1 are the low‑output versions of the 1.5‑litre three‑cylinder; B38A15M0/M1 are the higher‑output versions. B48A20M0/M1 are the 2.0‑litre four‑cylinder petrols; B48A20O0 is the high‑output 225i version.

overview.h2: BMW 2 Series Active Tourer F45 — Overview
overview.intro: The F45 is BMW's first front‑wheel‑drive production car — a five‑door, two‑row compact MPV launched in August 2014. It marked a significant shift for BMW, moving away from rear‑wheel drive to the UKL2 platform shared with the Mini Countryman, in order to maximise interior space and practicality. Designed to compete directly with the Mercedes‑Benz B‑Class, the Active Tourer offered a spacious cabin, sliding rear seats, and a premium feel. Under the bonnet, the F45 featured BMW's latest modular engine family — the B37, B38, B47 and B48 — across a range of petrol, diesel and plug‑in hybrid variants. A 2018 facelift brought revised styling and updated engine outputs. Production ran until 2021, when the second‑generation U06 replaced it.
overview.keyFacts: Platform: BMW UKL2 (front‑wheel drive) • Predecessor: None (first‑generation Active Tourer) • Successor: BMW U06 • Sibling body styles in this generation: F46 (Gran Tourer, three‑row MPV).
overview.marketIntelligenceLine: The F45 generates consistent UK enquiries [PATTERN DATA], concentrated on 218i petrol and 218d/220d diesel variants as these compact MPVs enter affordable‑maintenance territory.

items.quote: Most dependable: The B48 2.0‑litre four‑cylinder petrol (220i/225i) — BMW's modular four‑cylinder engine — offers strong performance, refinement, and proven durability in both Mini and BMW applications. The B38 1.5‑litre three‑cylinder petrol (218i) is also a solid, efficient choice for daily driving, delivering 134–138 bhp with excellent fuel economy. The B47 2.0‑litre diesel (218d/220d) is a robust, torque‑rich unit with strong fuel efficiency. The B37 1.5‑litre diesel (214d/216d) is efficient but less refined. The 225xe PHEV adds complexity but is generally reliable. Buyers should prioritise a well‑maintained B48 or B38 with documented service history.

ownershipEconomics.h2: BMW 2 Series Active Tourer F45 — Ownership Economics

rows.ownershipVerdict: Efficient Entry — smooth, cheap to run
Best Petrol Three — the sweet spot in the range
Smooth & Strong — the 2.0‑litre is a gem
Performance Pick — 228 bhp in an MPV
Best Diesel — the 2.0‑litre is the pick

economicsRule.title: The F45 rule
economicsRule.text: A £2,500–£4,800 B47 recon against a £5,000–£18,000 F45 sits at 14–96% of the car's value. On a sub‑£6,000 F45, that brushes our 70% BMW threshold — we'll honestly tell you to walk away. Against a well‑kept £12,000+ example, the same recon drops to a routine 20–40% — a repair decision, not a financial one. Value the car first.

problems.title: B38/B48 Water Pump & Cooling System Failure
problems.rootCause: The B38 and B48 engines share a common weak point — the water pump and cooling system components can fail, leading to coolant loss, overheating, and potential engine damage if not addressed promptly.
problems.recommendation: On any B38‑ or B48‑powered F45, budget for water pump inspection at 60k miles — a £200–£500 preventative replacement is cheap insurance against the £2,500–£4,500 replacement that follows a catastrophic overheat.

cta.label: Compare B38/B48 engine prices for your F45 →

problems.title: B47 Diesel DPF & EGR Clogging
problems.rootCause: The B47 diesel's DPF and EGR system clog with soot, particularly on urban‑use vehicles — leading to reduced power, increased fuel consumption, and eventual engine warning lights.
problems.recommendation: If your B47‑powered F45 is used primarily for short trips, a £150–£400 DPF and EGR service every 20k miles is cheap insurance against the £600–£1,200 replacement that follows.

cta.label: Compare B47 diesel DPF repair quotes →

problems.title: 225xe PHEV Battery Degradation
problems.rootCause: The 225xe's lithium‑ion battery pack can degrade over time, reducing electric‑only range and overall efficiency. Battery health is a key consideration for used PHEV buyers.
problems.recommendation: On any 225xe, have the battery health checked before purchase — a £100–£300 health check is far cheaper than the £2,500–£5,000 replacement that follows if the battery fails.

problems.title: F45 Tailgate Wiring Loom Failure (Non‑Engine)
problems.affectedModels: All F45 variants, particularly early 2014–2017 models.
problems.rootCause: Not an engine failure, but a common non‑engine issue on the F45 — the wiring loom in the tailgate hinge area can fracture, causing rear light failure, central locking issues, and boot release problems.
problems.recommendation: Have the tailgate wiring inspected on any F45 — it's a common age‑related failure that can cause MOT issues if left unaddressed.

problems.title: F45 Rear Subframe Corrosion (Non‑Engine)
problems.affectedModels: All F45 variants, particularly UK‑market cars.
problems.rootCause: Not an engine failure, but a known structural issue — the rear subframe mounting points can corrode, particularly in cars exposed to UK road salt.
problems.recommendation: Have the rear subframe mounts inspected on any F45 over 8 years old — it's a common MOT failure point on UK‑market cars.
